chunks.text: In the 1960 s
the poverty of developing countries was a theme that developed nations somewhat wanted to forget.
In many countries of Africa, Asia, and South America
hunger and disease caused people to suffer.
But an agricultural scientist stood up.
Norman Borlaug.
He led a movement called the " Green Revolution " and
through the improvement of wheat varieties, succeeded in dramatically increasing the world's food production.
The high - yield varieties he discovered grew even in areas with little rainfall.
In India and Mexico, wheat production doubled in a few years.
Hundreds of millions of lives were saved.
It was necessary to praise him aloud.
He received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1970.
The Green Revolution looked like a miracle, but there was also criticism.
Monoculture agriculture is vulnerable to pests.
The use of chemical fertilizers drains the soil and lowers productivity over the long term.
Borlaug himself faced these criticism sincerely.
" The Green Revolution is only a temporary victory," he said.
" As long as the population keeps growing, the food problem will not end.
Today, the world population has passed eight billion.
Without the achievements of the Green Revolution, hunger would be far worse.
But new challenges are piling up.
Climate change, depletion of water resources, soil exhaustion.
Next - generation technologies such as " gene - edited crops " are being developed.
But these technologies bring new ethical subject along with them.
Food security is the highest priority issue that every country's ministry should face with confidence.
We must pass the lesson Borlaug left us on to the next generation.
